title: WHERE & Operators

glossary.definition: Thrown away or rejected; in SQL, rows that do not meet a certain condition and are removed from the results.
Containing or including the endpoints or limits of a range, like counting from 1 to 10 and including both 1 and 10.
The state of belonging to a specific group or list, like checking if a country is in a specific list of countries.

overview.what:
WHERE filters the row-set that FROM produced, keeping only rows where the condition evaluates to TRUE. Any row where the condition is FALSE or NULL is discarded.

In the logical query flow: FROM builds the full row-set → WHERE removes rows that fail the test → SELECT picks the output columns.

Comparison operators — =, <>, <, >, <=, >= — compare a column to a value:

  SELECT name, price
  FROM products
  WHERE price > 50
  ORDER BY price;

Returns 3 rows: Chair (89.99), Desk (149.99), Monitor (199.99).

Logical operators — AND, OR, NOT — combine conditions:

  SELECT name, price
  FROM products
  WHERE category = 'Electronics' AND price < 50
  ORDER BY name;

Returns 2 rows: Keyboard (49.99), Mouse (19.99).

BETWEEN tests an inclusive range:

  SELECT name, price
  FROM products
  WHERE price BETWEEN 20 AND 100
  ORDER BY price;

Returns 3 rows: Keyboard (49.99), Chair (89.99) — equivalent to price >= 20 AND price <= 100. Mouse (19.99) is excluded because 19.99 < 20.

IN tests membership in a list:

  SELECT name, country
  FROM customers
  WHERE country IN ('US', 'UK')
  ORDER BY name;

Returns 4 rows: Alice, Bob, Diana, Greg.

LIKE matches text patterns (% = any characters, _ = one character):

  SELECT name
  FROM customers
  WHERE name LIKE 'A%'
  ORDER BY name;

Returns 1 row: Alice.

Key mental model: WHERE is a row-by-row TRUE/FALSE gate. It never changes values — it only decides which rows survive to the next stage.
overview.why: Almost every real query filters data. Without WHERE, every query returns every row in the table — useless for any application that needs specific records. WHERE is also critical for UPDATE and DELETE safety: forgetting it updates or deletes every row.

why_learning_this: Every filtered API list endpoint translates user parameters into WHERE conditions
Understanding WHERE's TRUE/FALSE/NULL gate explains why NULL comparisons silently drop rows — a critical prerequisite for NULL handling

common_mistakes.title: Using = NULL instead of IS NULL
common_mistakes.explanation: NULL is not a value — it means 'unknown'. The expression country = NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), so the row is silently dropped. Use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL instead.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing AND/OR precedence
common_mistakes.explanation: AND binds tighter than OR. The condition A OR B AND C is read as A OR (B AND C), not (A OR B) AND C. Use parentheses to make intent explicit.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends
common_mistakes.explanation: BETWEEN 20 AND 100 includes both 20 and 100. If you want to exclude endpoints, use > and < instead.

recall_questions.q: At what stage in the logical query flow does WHERE run — before or after SELECT?
recall_questions.answer: WHERE runs after FROM but before SELECT. It filters the row-set that FROM produced, and SELECT then picks columns from the surviving rows.

recall_questions.q: What happens when a WHERE condition evaluates to NULL for a row?
recall_questions.answer: The row is discarded. WHERE keeps only rows where the condition is TRUE — both FALSE and NULL rows are removed.

recall_questions.q: What do the % and _ wildcards mean in a LIKE pattern?
recall_questions.answer: % matches any sequence of zero or more characters. _ matches exactly one character.

understanding_checks.query:
SELECT name, country
FROM customers
WHERE country = 'US' OR country = 'MX'
ORDER BY name;
understanding_checks.question: Which customers does this return?
understanding_checks.why: WHERE tests each row: country = 'US' is TRUE for Alice, Bob, Greg; country = 'MX' is TRUE for Carlos. OR keeps a row if either condition is TRUE. Emma's NULL country fails both tests (NULL = 'US' is NULL, not TRUE).

understanding_checks.query:
SELECT name, price
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics' AND price > 100
ORDER BY name;
understanding_checks.question: What rows does this return?
understanding_checks.why: AND requires both conditions to be TRUE. Of the 3 Electronics products (Keyboard 49.99, Mouse 19.99, Monitor 199.99), only Monitor has price > 100.

query_walkthrough.query:
SELECT name, price
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics' AND price < 50
ORDER BY name;
query_walkthrough.focus: Watch WHERE discard 4 of the 6 product rows — only rows where BOTH conditions are TRUE survive to SELECT.

practice_tasks.title: Widen the filter to include Furniture
practice_tasks.prompt: This query returns only Electronics products under $50. Modify the WHERE clause so it also includes products in the 'Furniture' category (at any price). Keep the same columns and ordering.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
SELECT name, price
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics' AND price < 50
ORDER BY name;
practice_tasks.solution:
SELECT name, price
FROM products
WHERE (category = 'Electronics' AND price < 50) OR category = 'Furniture'
ORDER BY name;

challenge.title: Orders in a date range
challenge.prompt: Write a query that returns the id and order_date of all orders placed between '2023-03-01' and '2023-06-30' (inclusive) with status 'paid'. Order by order_date.
challenge.solution:
SELECT id, order_date
FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2023-03-01' AND '2023-06-30'
  AND status = 'paid'
ORDER BY order_date;
